data.title: OXLC Vs. ECC: Ditching Oxford Lane For Eagle Point's Stability
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Oxford Lane Capital Corporation (OXLC) and Eagle Point Credit Company (ECC) are two direct CEF competitors that specialize in the niche market of investing in equity tranches (stocks of issuers that make payments last, once all of the issuer's bondholders have received their interest) and junior debt instruments of Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLO). I will conduct a comparative analysis between OXLC and ECC in this article, and the results demonstrate significant risks when investing in these assets. Although the yield on these funds is quite high (32.63% and 28.97%), changes in their shares over the past year have been extremely negative (-42.2% and -34.09%, respectively). In addition, the expense ratios of 12.45% and 9.53% imposed on investors will only exacerbate the situation to the detriment of your investments.

Forecast thesis for 2026

The CLO market is showing interesting dynamics: spreads on B-rated loans are narrowing, and funds such as OXLC are facing the problem of limited upside potential. At the same time, under the same conditions, a fund like ECC has additional potential due to the fact that its portfolio contains a sufficient share of debt tranches. In the end, in 2026, OXLC and ECC's total return results may diverge more significantly than they did before. Indeed, the bet on aggressive growth in Oxford's distribution may be met with a forced significant reduction in the payments themselves. If this happens, investing in its shares will become unprofitable for those who, seeking high passive returns, risk sacrificing their capital to a persistent trend of erosion. In contrast, ECC offers greater structural discipline, coupled with greater diversification of its asset portfolio, making it better prepared for changes in spreads.

Moreover, in the second half of 2025, market risks in the US economy increased due to higher default rates. The 12-month rolling default rate, however, increased but remained at 1.47%. Additionally, the ECC's report for Q3 2025 indicated that their default risk was 0.34%, much better than the overall market level. All this despite the fact that there were five major defaults in the US in Q3 2025, among them First Brands. With OXLC, their portfolio takes on broader market risk, allowing for a higher NII, while at the same time having a more sensitive NAV in relation to changes in the level of defaults in the economy.

The reason I'm interested in analyzing OXLC and ECC is to pick the more reliable asset, because I think the default rate in the US will probably go up in 2026 (mainly because of economic turbulence caused by domestic and international political decisions). Moreover, according to Fitch Ratings forecasts, the default rate on loans will range from 4.5% to 5%, with high-yield bonds ranging from 2.5% to 3%. My opinion is that the forecast default rate for 2026 should be set at around 2% to 2.5%. Under these circumstances, ECC is expected to maintain its NAV better than the OXLC. Nonetheless, ECC management has emphasized that due to the Fed's rate cuts, defaults should be lower than the market expects, since the cost of servicing debt for borrowers will be lower.

Fundamental similarities and differences between OXLC and ECC

OXLC and ECC have a number of characteristics that make them similar for many investors.

  • The first is their narrow focus on the lower end of the credit market through their work with CLOs. More specifically, funds buy equity tranches, who are less liquid and reliable because their ratings may be below investment grade.
  • The second is that their investment model is based on earning money from the arbitrage difference between the interest received on loans and the cost of servicing CLO debt.
  • Finally, they both have very high dividend payments (roughly 30% per year), so they're high-yield assets, plus the dividends are paid out every month.

In connection with the specifics of managing the assets and liabilities of these funds, there is approximately the same behavior of their NAV. With OXLC and ECC, there is a dynamic of constant capital erosion, as characterized by the fact that the funds try to squeeze out as much of the available funds to generate high returns for payments to shareholders. The erosion of OXLC's NAV over the past 5 years has exceeded 40%.

With ECC, the NAV chart is more volatile, while capital erosion over the past five years has amounted to approximately 50%. While Eagle Point's external management allows the fund to review the terms of its CLOs more frequently in order to extend their life span, thereby maintaining their previous value, that does not help solve the problem of NAV erosion.

That said, the fundamental similarities between OXLC and ECC end there. If we dig deeper into these funds, there are some key differences that set them apart.

One of the main differences is how they manage their liabilities (or, in simple terms, where they get their money from). These are critical parameters of their business, as the spread between interest rates depends on them. For OXLC, raising funds involves issuing two types of securities: preferred shares and fixed-rate "baby bonds." Bond issuance is long-term, making it possible to reliably fix the specified interest rate. This allows the fund to attract funds at a lower cost, resulting in higher profits when market interest rates rise. Their income from CLOs is based on floating rates. When the cycle reverses and rates fall, however, then OXLC finds this asset management model unprofitable. ECC tends to use preferred shares and "baby bonds" in a similar way. The fund's issuance policy is more flexible, so it conducts additional issuances more often. During periods of falling rates, the additional issuance of securities allows the fund to reduce the cost of attracted funds, thereby maintaining a more stable spread between rates.

An important difference between OXLC and ECC is their portfolio composition, with the former fund having almost 100% of its assets concentrated in CLO Equity (for the last year, its share has fluctuated between 97% and 99%), while ECC's share is 77% according to the latest data, and the rest is held in other assets, including CLO Debt 2%, Loan Accumulation Facilities 3%, Regulatory Capital Relief 3%, Consumer ABS 5%, Collateralized Fund Obligations 4%, and other assets. In the case of the OXLC portfolio, their assets are the riskiest, since they are the first to bear the losses from defaults. On the other hand, if excess income is generated, it is the first to benefit. Therefore, it can be concluded that the OXLC portfolio is more volatile and has a higher beta to the credit market. With 23% of its portfolio in other assets, ECC has a kind of "safety cushion" that allows it to offset some of the risks from CLO Equity during periods of market turbulence.

Also, between OXLC and ECC, there are differences related to their dividend policies, though the payout yields are roughly equal at 32.63% and 28.97%, respectively. This difference isn't in the percentages themselves or when they're paid out, though, rather in how the funds' income distribution methods differ. The OXLC has no capital return risk, for example, because 100% of its dividends are ordinary income. As a result, the tax efficiency for investors is lower. There is moderate capital return risk with the ECC, though the fund's managers aim to ensure that dividends are real income from portfolio management. According to the latest payments for December 2025, ROC accounted for 15.48%. In one way, it makes these distributions more tax-efficient. But on the other hand, this increases the risk of capital erosion.

OXLC and ECC comparison table

A comparison table based on key criteria is provided below to help you determine the difference between OXLC and ECC. Both qualitative indicators and quantitative metrics are reflected here.

Given that the key difference between any funds is reflected in their Total Return indicators, let's take a look at the difference between them. For this purpose, let's analyze the period over the last year, during which ECC had a higher Total Return, with a negative value of -16.43%, whereas OXLC had a negative value of -23.59%. This trend hasn't always been the case, because historically, over 10 years, Total Return is higher for OXLC, amounting to 105.75% compared to 88.48% for ECC.

Risks of investing in CEFs

Investing in CEFs such as OXLC and ECC involves significant risks. It is worth highlighting the following types of risks that every investor should be aware of:

  • "First loss" risk, because these funds invest in CLOs. These are not the most reliable segments of the credit market. If defaults begin to rise in the economy, it will be those who invest in CLOs who will be the first to lose their money.
  • Risk of "hidden" defaults, as highlighted by Moody’s and S&P analysts in 2026. It refers to a process where borrowers restructure their loans, giving some creditors priority over others outside the standard bankruptcy framework. This can cause assets within CLOs to depreciate sharply, whereas the default rate remains low.
  • There is a risk of spreads narrowing, because the Fed's rate-cutting cycle could cause the yield on assets in fund portfolios to decline faster than the cost of servicing their debt.
  • The risks of operating expenses, who are still really high. This means that any further erosion of capital and possible reduction in dividends will lead to total and real returns for investors being too low or, more than that, unprofitable.
  • High leverage risk, as using leverage at 30-40% of assets could cause the NAV of funds to fall even faster when the market declines. My estimate is that this beta is somewhere between 1.5x and 2.0x.

Conclusion

Therefore, based on the results of a comparative analysis between OXLC and ECC, it is possible to identify differences between them, due to which I prefer ECC, because this CEF has a more balanced portfolio structure, that should better protect it from narrowing interest rate spreads. For this reason, I recommend a "Hold" rating for this fund, whereas I assign a "Sell" rating to OXLC. Based on the principle of capital preservation for an income-oriented portfolio, such assets cannot be considered suitable due to the constant erosion of NAV. But in certain market cycles, which cause the discount to increase to a whopping 20-25%, it becomes an interesting instrument to buy in the short and medium term. Simplifying the above, one could say that OXLC is a cash flow generator, whereas ECC is a bet on a smarter game with the CLO market. Sacrificing a few percentage points of return, the manager of the latter fund managed to preserve its capital more stably in 2025, something that cannot be said about OXLC.

data.title: abrdn Emerging Markets Dividend Active ETF Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

The Fund rose by 4.95% over the quarter, outpacing the benchmark by 16 basis points.

The AI-driven rally lifted several of our tech holdings. In Taiwan, semiconductor chip testing manufacturer Chroma ATE rallied on expectations that the company will continue to benefit from accelerating demand for its products and services, with improving profit margins supported by strong pricing power. ASE Technology, the world’s largest pure- play semiconductor assembly and test service provider, also fared well, buoyed by rising demand for AI applications. Similarly in South Korea, memory chip producers Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix were major contributors at the stock level.

On the flip side, Chinese stocks sold off as investors took profit amid softer domestic growth and the underwhelming government response. Consumer-related stock Tencent came under pressure, despite its robust prospects, though the relatively light exposure to Alibaba proved constructive. Anta Sports, an apparel and sportswear provider, retreated on the back of lower-than-expected sales growth, partially attributed to warmer weather which dampened seasonal demand for autumn/winter clothing lines. Video game developer NetEase also underperformed amid a decline in mobile game revenues, though its overseas business showed encouraging momentum. Separately, Sands China, an operator of casino and hotel resorts in Macau, was weighed down by weaker-than-expected gaming revenue growth in December, following a strong recovery in growth earlier in the year.

Market review

Emerging market equities ended the quarter higher and outperformed developed markets, led by a near 28% jump in South Korea. China, however, lagged relative to peers.

Technology stocks rallied, driven by semiconductors, specifically memory stocks, as confirmed chip shortages resulted in noticeable price increases for DRAM (dynamic random-access memory) and HBM (high-bandwidth memory) chips. This proved particularly beneficial for South Korean heavyweights and leading memory chip producers.

In the US, following interest rate cuts in September and October, the Federal Reserve reduced rates again in December and signalled further easing in 2026. While economic data remains mixed, growing concerns over the US labour market saw the central bank move more in line with President Donald Trump’s desire for lower rates. The market’s response was positive as the easing cycle has come without a recession, which bodes well for emerging market risk assets.

Commodities such as gold, silver and copper experienced strong demand as hedges against policy risk and for industrial use, which benefitted resource-heavy Latin American markets and South Africa. Geopolitical tensions, policy uncertainty as well as US monetary policy easing have increased demand for gold and silver, while copper and other minerals have gained from artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure development, electric vehicle adoption and energy transition.

Activity

Over the quarter, we sold the iShares MSCI India ETF and rebalanced our India holdings as we invested directly in the local market following our account opening. Among the key introductions in India were Power Grid Corporation of India, Bajaj Holdings & Investment and Tata Consultancy Services.

We also established a number of new positions elsewhere in view of their attractive prospects. Purchases in Asia included aluminium smelter Chuangxin Industries and integrated microelectronics company NAURA Technology in China, as well as South Korean power plant maintenance service provider KEPCO Plant Service & Engineering, which broadens our exposure to rising nuclear power demand

globally. In Brazil, we bought independent oil exploration and production company Prio, pulp and paper producer Suzano, and electrical transformer and motors company WEG.

We also added Emaar Properties, a property development company in the United Arab Emirates that is underpinned by robust cash flow generation, a multi-year order backlog and a net cash balance sheet.

Conversely, we sold Classys, Delta Electronics, PDD Holdings (PDD), Tube Investments of India and Vale to fund better opportunities elsewhere.

Outlook

Markets remain centred on hopes around the future of AI. This is despite continuing geopolitical risks linked to Trump’s transactional use of the world’s most powerful office and retreat from global security commitments, and rising risks of declining fiscal discipline in other developed markets.

Overall, Trump’s tariffs suggest that his goals of shifting manufacturing and raising revenue for tax cuts are front and centre. Market consensus continues to see a move towards a breakdown in China-US trade, though the pace and extent of decoupling remain a key uncertainty. We believe China will continue to use its leverage to arrive at some accord. Trump has also extended tariff pressure to India and Brazil, but we expect an agreement to reduce tariffs will be reached. However, the timing is hard to predict given domestic politics and populist leaders.

We remain constructive on the outlook for emerging markets, supported by a weaker US dollar and lower rates. Our investment focus is on three structural developments - technology as a platform, infrastructure and domestic brands - which we expect to drive earnings growth in emerging markets. Valuations remain relatively attractive, though we are mindful of broader risks. We expect the Fund’s balanced exposure to high dividend and dividend growth businesses to support performance both in periods of market dislocation and greater optimism.

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, fees, charges, and expenses of an ETF before investing. The summary and full prospectuses contain this and other information about the ETF and should be read carefully before investing. data.title: Community Financial System: Still Offering Growth At A Reasonable Price
data.description:
The past few months have been pretty good for Community Financial System (CBU). Balance sheet repricing dynamics remain highly favorable for the Ithaca-based bank, and that has been great for its interest margin and earnings. At the same time, buoyant financial markets have been a boon for some of Community's fee-based businesses. All of this has given the stock a nice lift, with shares returning around 10% since my last update in November.

Rating it "Buy" in that piece, I liked Community's margin-driven growth prospects. I also liked the fact that its valuation looked reasonable compared to its historical average. The company just reported its Q4 2025 results, so now is a good opportunity to reassess these points. I remain bullish, with Community still offering a nice combination of growth and value.

Favorable Repricing Dynamics

Like I said, Community has been reporting strong earnings growth of late. The company just posted $1.12 in operating EPS for Q4 2025, 12% higher than the year before.

Much of this growth is coming from a wider net interest margin. Community's NIM was 3.37% last quarter, 7 basis points higher than when I last wrote on the company. After bottoming in Q1 2024, the company has now seen seven consecutive quarters of sequential expansion.

The ongoing repricing of Community's fixed-rate assets explains much of this. The bank has over $4 billion in investment securities, mainly consisting of U.S. Treasuries, and these were mostly purchased before interest rates rose back in Q1 2022. Reflecting this, Community's investment yield was just 2.14% last quarter. With the 10-year Treasury yield over 4.2% as of this writing, the upward repricing of these securities is a major source of growth.

Now, Community isn't actually reinvesting maturities back into more Treasury bonds. That's why its investment yield hasn't improved much. In fact, despite a whole year of favorable interest rates, the investment yield actually fell 1bp year-on-year.

Instead, Community is using some of the proceeds to fund loan growth. The bank had $10.95 billion in loans on its books at the end of 2025, up around $500 million from Q4 2024. Because new loans are still going on at relatively high yields, Community's blended loan yield hasn't fallen. In fact, despite the decline in short-term interest rates, it was flat quarter-on-quarter at 5.68%. In turn, that has kept Community's overall asset yield relatively stable (it rose 1bp sequentially last quarter to 4.60%).

Community is also using the run-off from its bond portfolio to retire its more expensive forms of funding. For instance, the bank had $463 million in FHLB and other borrowings on its balance sheet last quarter. This was down from over $530 million in the previous quarter. As a result, Community has managed to squeeze some more gains out of its already-low funding costs. The bank was paying 1.27% on its funding sources last quarter, down 6bps sequentially.

There are a few additional points I would make. Firstly, although it has risen strongly since 2024, Community's NIM remains depressed compared to historical levels. Indeed, while the banking industry's NIM exceeded its 2015-2019 average back in Q3 2025, Community's Q4 2025 NIM was still 35bps below its 2015-2019 average (~3.72%).

Secondly, given how low its investment yield still is, this trend of favorable asset repricing should continue for the foreseeable future. Like recent quarters, this should lead to more NIM gains in 2026 and beyond.

Finally, this growth is particularly lucrative for Community, as a higher NIM doesn't really attract any incremental expenses. In other words, it mostly drops to pre-tax earnings, meaning Community's earnings growth should continue to be strong.

Elevated Asset Markets Also Helping

While NIM expansion is the main reason I am bullish on Community's prospects, the company has also benefited from favorable trends elsewhere. Before elaborating, it's important to point out that Community's revenue is unusually diverse for a small bank. Net interest income still accounts for most of its top line, but non-interest income is a relatively high 40% of the mix.

This revenue mainly comes from the bank's Employee Benefits Services and Wealth Management Services segments. Wealth Management should be self-explanatory, while the Employee Benefits business involves things like administering workplace retirement plans. Around half of revenue within the Employee Benefits segment is priced off asset levels, rising to around 90% for Wealth Management.

Given a portion of its administered/managed assets will be in equities, Community's asset-based income has seen support from buoyant stock markets. As readers will know, 2025 was another strong year for the S&P 500, which returned nearly 20% since the start of that year. Given comps early in the year, this should continue to be a modest tailwind for Community in 2026.

Similar to what I said in the last section, higher asset-based fees should also be accretive to Community's profit margins. And on top of the tailwind coming from NIM expansion, this explains why the bank's earnings are growing nicely.

Credit Quality Still Fine

While I have principally been interested in Community's NIM expansion, I did like its history of strong credit quality as well, as the bank has historically recorded fewer loan losses than the industry.

Credit quality trends remain stable. Net charge-offs were just 0.09% of total loans last quarter, down 3bps year-on-year and flat quarter-on-quarter. Non-performing loans have performed similarly, falling 20bps year-on-year to 0.52% (flat quarter-on-quarter). Given asset quality trends have been relatively stable across the industry, I am not expecting higher credit charges to threaten Community's earnings in 2026.

Reasonably Priced

Community earned $4.24 per share in operating net income last year. Its shares trade for $61.77 as I type, for a multiple of 14.6 times trailing EPS. As you can see, this is roughly in line with Community's historical average.

While this wouldn't suggest its shares are cheap, Community's NIM-driven growth still has a lot of room to run. Like I said earlier, its NIM remains around 35bps below its 2015-2019 average. Given Community has $15.7 billion in interest-earnings assets on its balance sheet, a move back to this NIM should result in significant incremental profits. The sell-side analysts that cover the bank seem to agree. Per Seeking Alpha, they forecast $4.83 per share in operating net income this year, growing to $5.48 per share in 2027.

If the stock simply held its current multiple, investors would be looking at a circa 13.5% annualized price return. Adding Community's dividend, which currently yields 3%, this would rise to around 16.5%. That gives investors a healthy margin of safety to clear a 10%-plus annual return. For that reason, I remain bullish.

data.title: The $530 Billion AI Question: Which Big Tech Stock Is Winning?
data.description:
Big Tech is expected to invest $530 billion for building AI infrastructure in 2026, while the path to near-term monetization remains a question mark. As investor scrutiny around capital expenditure intensifies, the key question is no longer who is spending the most on AI, but who is translating that spend into measurable revenue and sustainable margins.

The market is right to question the shift in role for Big Tech, as these companies have swiftly moved from global demand engines for consumer and enterprise software to now being large consumers themselves of a rather expensive endeavour.

When asked about Big Tech for the last few years, my answer has been consistent – my firm is heavily positioned in the suppliers. For example, while many are celebrating Google’s stock performance last year, stocks like AMD and Micron effortlessly outperformed the 2025 Big Tech winner – as did dozens of lesser-known suppliers. This is easily visible in the QQQs returning 20.2% last year compared to SMH at 48.7%.

But I digress, as although Big Tech has paled in stock returns compared to select AI semiconductor stocks, my firm is anticipating an incoming monetization wave driven by inference. The moment that Big Tech and others can effectively monetize their large investments will unfold in the coming years. Big Tech is not muted; it’s just a bit later in the cycle given they’re predominately software companies.

My firm strives to be early to trends, positioning in cloud in 2019 ahead of Covid, then began rotating out of cloud to position for AI in late 2022 around extreme lows and ahead of Nvidia’s Hopper release. We then added AI energy names to our portfolio ahead of the Street in February – April of 2025 (again) before the broad market and around extreme lows.

On a similar note, I fully expect we will be well-positioned for the moment AI can finally monetize – which brings us back to Big Tech.

The report below compares Big Tech companies across revenue, AI segment revenue, margins, capex intensity, and balance sheet health, to help assess which companies are best positioned to convert AI investment into durable growth and profits. With infrastructure spending at historic highs, the stakes have never been higher.

Microsoft ($MSFT): Azure Acceleration Powered by AI

Microsoft has consistently delivered double-digit revenue growth across all three quarters of 2025, driven by strong demand for cloud and AI.

  • The company’s Q3 revenue grew by 18.4% YoY to $77.67 billion.
  • Analysts expect revenue to grow 15.3% YoY to $80.3 billion in Q4, representing more than 300 bps deceleration due to seasonality and the revenue decelerated 370 bps in the same period last year.

Yet under the hood, Microsoft Azure is witnessing accelerating demand, rising 40% in Q3, driven by better-than-expected growth in the company’s core infrastructure business, primarily from its largest customers and AI-demand. The company’s productivity and business processes segment has also demonstrated steady, balanced growth driven by continued adoption of Microsoft 365.

The last direct update on AI revenue we got from Microsoft is from fiscal Q2 ending December 2024, where AI revenue was stated to be $13 billion, growing at a pace of 175% year-over-year. Microsoft did denote in fiscal Q3 that AI services contributed 16 points to Azure’s 35% YoY growth, up from 13 points in the prior quarter.

Assuming AI drove Azure’s reacceleration from 35% to 39% in the past two quarters, the base case for Microsoft assumes AI contributing approximately 22 points to growth as of the prior quarter, fiscal Q1, or around 56% of its YoY growth in dollars. This could imply AI revenue at 26% of Azure’s total revenue, or around $25 to $26 billion on a nearly $100 billion annual run rate for Azure. Microsoft has also struck a new deal with OpenAI under which it has committed to $250 billion of compute capacity through 2032, and also signed a deal with Anthropic for $30 billion of compute capacity.

The company has consistently delivered strong profits, driven by higher software and cloud services revenue, and operational efficiencies. The company’s Q3 operating margin improved by 400 basis points sequentially and 230 basis points YoY to 48.9%, driven by operating leverage and higher-margin revenue.

Microsoft’s capex in Q3 was $34.9 billion, an increase of 75% YoY from $20 billion in the year-ago quarter. The company’s strong capex growth was primarily driven by increasing demand for its Cloud and AI offerings. With strong accelerating demand, Microsoft is increasing its spending on GPUs and CPUs. Therefore, total spending is expected to increase sequentially in the next quarter. The company maintains a strong balance sheet with a net cash position of $58.8 billion.

Takeaway: Given Microsoft’s large capex spending, the market is likely to be unconvinced in the near-term the AI contribution to Azure is happening quickly enough. If our math is correct, AI’s contribution is less than 1/5 of this year’s capex and we are years deep now – meaning its much lower in terms of contribution to cumulative AI capex.

Keep in mind, Microsoft has publicly stated they will absorb grid costs for their AI infrastructure to avoid passing it off to consumers – which is ethically the right thing to do – yet this increases total capex cost per GW. Recently, Microsoft President Brad Smith stated: “We will pay utility rates that are high enough to cover our electricity costs in part by collaborating with utilities on plans to add the electricity supply that we will need.” It’s likely other Big Tech companies will follow suit.

Google ($GOOGL): Record 83% Capex Surge to Scale Search and Google Cloud Momentum

Google’s Q3 revenue grew by 16.2% YoY to $102.35 billion. Analysts expect Q4 revenue to grow 15.4% YoY to $111.3 billion. The company is witnessing momentum across its business segments with Google Cloud being a standout, with accelerating revenue growth from 28% in Q1 to 32% in Q2, and further accelerating to 34% YoY growth to $15.2 billion. This has been primarily driven by Google Cloud Platform, Generative AI Solutions, and AI Infrastructure.

The Cloud business continues its trajectory of accelerated growth, with AI-driven revenue emerging as a key catalyst. Cloud backlog increased 46% QoQ to $155 billion, underscoring strong demand visibility and sustained momentum across enterprise deployments. Most importantly, over 70% of existing Google Cloud customers use the company’s AI products.

Google said that its enterprise AI products in GCP are generating ‘billions’ in quarterly revenue but provided no specific breakout beyond that, and also did not provide an update on Search. However, assuming a similar split as Microsoft for AI contribution in the latest quarter, such as ~17 points or half of growth, this would project quarterly AI revenue to be ~$1.9 billion, or nearly $8 billion annualized, less than one-third of Microsoft’s current run rate. Google signed a deal with Anthropic in October said to be worth tens of billions, giving the Claude parent access to up to one million TPUs, bringing more than 1GW of capacity online in 2026.

AI’s contribution to Search revenue could be larger, assuming that the acceleration from 10% YoY in Q1 to 15% YoY in Q3 is entirely driven by AI and AI Overviews, or 5 points. Search growth was ~12-13% in the second half of 2024 after the launch of AI Overviews and assuming ~2 point contribution from AI by Q4, this would roughly estimate AI’s contribution to Search at $800 million. As of Q3, under that 5 point assumption, AI’s contribution would be estimated at roughly $2.3-2.4 billion, or closing in on a $10 billion annual run rate.

Google’s Q3 operating margin improved 180 basis points YoY and 160 basis points QoQ to 34.1%, driven by operational efficiencies. Google’s high-margin advertising business remains the core profit engine, delivering durable and resilient earnings growth.

Google reported the fastest capex growth among Big Tech in Q3. The company’s capex grew by 83% YoY to $23.95 billion. Sequentially, it grew by 7% from $22.4 billion in the previous quarter. Management stated in the Q3 earnings call that they are witnessing positive returns on AI investments. “I would say it's not just early signs because we're seeing returns, obviously, in the Cloud business. You've heard us talk about the fact that we already are generating billions of dollars from AI in the quarter.”

Looking ahead, the company expects to invest aggressively due to strong demand from cloud customers and growth opportunities across the company. Management expects 2025 capex to be in the range of $91 billion to $93 billion, up from the previous estimate of $85 billion. It represents YoY growth of 75% at the midpoint. Capex is expected to increase further in 2026, which supports our view that AI stocks will benefit. Google’s balance sheet remains robust, with a net cash balance of $76.9 billion, the highest among Big Tech companies.

Takeaway:

Google reported the strongest returns last year across Big Tech, likely due to a combination of Google Cloud accelerating alongside an easier monetization with Search given advertising can see an immediate impact from automation. The company repeatedly emphasizes that integrating AI into Search improves advertiser outcomes for more effective campaigns, therefore, investors should keep an eye on the Search inflection as much (if not more so) than Google Cloud.

In April 2025, Google introduced Ironwood v7, its first TPU designed specifically for inference. Another impetus for 2025’s strong performance is using its own silicon to drive inference at a lower cost curve, which will result in improved unit economics for Search and GCP. We discussed in detail the Google TPU Ironwood v7 rollout in our recent report to our free newsletter subscribers.

Amazon ($AMZN): AWS Reported the Fastest Growth since Q4 2022

Amazon has the second slowest revenue growth among the Big Tech companies. The company’s Q3 revenue grew by 13.4% YoY to $180.17 billion. Revenue is expected to grow by 12.4% YoY to $211.14 billion in Q4. Q3 AWS revenue grew by 20.2% YoY to $33 billion, accelerating by 270 basis points from 17.5% growth in Q2. The company reported the fastest AWS growth since Q4 2022, driven by strong demand in AI and core infrastructure.

Amazon has provided a few hints on its AI revenue, saying in Q2 that AI was a “fast-growing triple-digit year-over-year percentage multibillion-dollar business” and in Q3 that its Trainium chips had grown 150% QoQ and were a multi-billion dollar business. Again, assuming AI contributed roughly half or slightly above half of AWS’ 20% YoY growth, or 10 to 12 points, this would project AI revenue to be ~$2.8-3.3 billion, or more than $10-12 billion annualized. This would represent 8-10% of AWS’ TTM revenue. Amazon also signed a $38 billion, multi-year deal with OpenAI in November to give the AI firm immediate access to Nvidia GPUs on AWS.

Amazon has the weakest margins among Big Tech companies, as the majority of its revenue is generated by its lower-margin e-commerce business. In Q3, operating margin improved modestly by 20 basis points YoY but declined 30 basis points sequentially to 11.3%.

Amazon is the biggest spender among the Big Tech companies. Amazon’s capex in Q3 rose 55% YoY to $35.1 billion, with the company raising the 2025 capex guidance to $125 billion, up 51% YoY. The company’s CEO, Andy Jassy, said in the Q3 earnings call, “You're going to see us continue to be very aggressive investing in capacity because we see the demand. As fast as we're adding capacity right now, we're monetizing it.” The company has a net cash position of $43.5 billion.

Takeaway:

AWS enjoyed an early mover advantage to cloud infrastructure-as-a-service (IaaS) yet that advantage could be in jeopardy in the AI era. These concerns were alleviated somewhat in the last earnings report as AWS revenue reported 20% YoY growth for $33 billion in Q3, marking the fastest growth since Q4 2022. Amazon also struck a seven-year cloud computing deal with OpenAI, signaling renewed momentum.

Similar to Google, AWS is building out its custom Trainium chip and expanded its AI-focused programs, including accelerators for startups and new cloud services that help enterprises adopt generative AI. The custom chips offer 4X performance and efficiency gains for better price-performance compared to GPUs. This helps to lower AWS’ compute costs and will lead to better margins given the more competitive pricing for training and inference at scale.

Meta ($META): The $60B Advantage+ Engine

Meta has the fastest growth among the Big Tech companies and is an early AI beneficiary. The company is benefiting from AI-driven recommendation models that are improving advertiser ROI and increasing time spent across its family of apps, supporting stronger advertising revenue growth. The company’s Q3 advertising revenue grew by 25.6% YoY to $50.1 billion, accelerating more than nine points since Q1 and marking the fastest growth in six quarters.

In terms of AI-driven revenue, Meta’s Advantage+ is outpacing OpenAI by 3X and is also offering the strongest AI revenue among the FAAMGs – in Q3, the company revealed that its end-to-end AI-powered ads platform Advantage+ has reached a $60 billion annual run rate, just 3.5 years after its launch. We recently covered this in more detail in our free newsletter, The AI Revenue Leader Nobody Is Talking About—Second Only to Nvidia Stock.

Meta is witnessing a deceleration in margins due to rising expenses supporting its AI infrastructure. Reality Labs also continues to incur losses, recording a $4.43 billion loss from operations in Q3 2025, and its cumulative losses now total $73.04 billion. During Q3 earnings, management mentioned that total expenses “will grow at a significantly faster percentage rate in 2026” driven by infrastructure costs, incremental cloud costs and depreciation, followed by employee compensation. In December, Meta’s CEO, Mark Zuckerberg, said that they plan to cut Metaverse projects by up to 30%, which is positive.

Meta’s Q3 capex grew by 111% YoY to $19.4 billion. The strong growth was primarily driven by investments in servers, data centers, and network infrastructure. Management also increased the 2025 capex to a range of $70 billion to $72 billion, up from the prior outlook of $66 billion to $72 billion. It represents a YoY growth of 81% from the prior year. Due to continued investments in AI infrastructure, Meta expects next year’s capex to be significantly higher than in 2025, particularly as their compute needs are higher than their expectations. Management stated in the earnings call, “As we have begun to plan for next year, it's become clear that our compute needs have continued to expand meaningfully, including versus our own expectations last quarter. We are still working through our capacity plans for next year, but we expect to invest aggressively to meet these needs, both by building our own infrastructure and contracting with third-party cloud providers.”

Meta has the weakest balance sheet among the Big Tech companies with a net cash position of $15.7 billion. Meta has also entered a joint venture with Blue Owl Capital to fund its development of the Hyperion data center in Louisiana. Thereby, helping it to keep about $27 billion in debt off-balance sheet, where it would sit in a special-purpose vehicle tied to Blue Owl. While this approach may improve reported leverage and financial ratios, it carries inherent risks.

Takeaway:

Which company has the world’s most valuable data? While some will argue it’s a tie between Google, Meta, Microsoft and Amazon – I believe the contextual richness of social media data puts Meta at a slight advantage. In addition, Advantage+ is the second largest AI revenue segment on the market today; a stat this is materially underreported in the media.

However, the biggest hurdle Meta faces is not visibly seen in the fundamentals but rather the company must convince developers to adopt its large language models, which are underwhelming on benchmarks compared to OpenAI and Anthropic's Claude. From where it stands today, it’s hard to see a path where Meta commercializes Llama.

Instead, in the near-term, Meta’s opportunity lies in its ability to monetize its own platforms. This approach has proven to be unusually effective, given the revenue we are seeing from Advantage+. Over time, however, Meta will need to bring additional AI-driven applications to market to keep pace with competitors that are beginning to monetize not only infrastructure, but the full software stack built on top of it.

Apple ($AAPL): Record Services Revenue

Apple has the slowest revenue growth among Big Tech companies. The company’s Q3 revenue grew by 7.9% YoY to $102.5 billion. Revenue is expected to accelerate to 11.4% YoY to $138.5 billion in Q4. The services revenue achieved a record of $28.8 billion in Q3, growing 15.1% YoY.

The company’s Q3 operating margin improved 50 basis points YoY and 170 basis points sequentially to 31.7%. The improvement in margins was primarily driven by favorable product mix, cost discipline, and a higher contribution of margin from better-margin services revenue.

Takeaway:

Apple is not an infrastructure player, and thus given we are in the infrastructure phase of AI, one of the world’s most valuable companies is largely sitting this one out. While Apple benefits from exceptional margins, a fortress balance sheet, and strong cash generation, those strengths are not translating into leadership in the current phase of the AI training market.

Our firm is focused on identifying AI’s biggest winners, and from that perspective, the opportunity cost of owning Apple over companies supplying AI infrastructure, energy, and on the brink of monetizing LLMs is significant. Apple may serve as a defensive allocation, but in my opinion, it does not offer the same asymmetric upside as the companies building—and monetizing—the backbone of the AI economy.

This may change once more AI applications are in the hands of consumers, but for now, Apple is not part of our AI investment focus.

Conclusion

Based on this analysis, Google stands out as having the strongest Big Tech AI positioning today, given its ability to monetize AI directly through Search while simultaneously accelerating Cloud growth. To add to this, Google will see improved unit economics with custom silicon with a path to expand margins despite elevated capex.

At the same time, we continue to see Big Tech broadly underperform AI hardware suppliers – even Google. For example, while many are celebrating Google’s stock performance last year, stocks like AMD and Micron effortlessly outperformed the 2025 Big Tech winner – as did dozens of lesser-known suppliers. This is easily visible in the QQQs returning 20.2% last year compared to SMH at 48.7%.

In our view, the most compelling value creation in today’s AI market remains on the supply side, particularly among underappreciated hardware and infrastructure players. Rather than Big Tech, Tech Insider Network is focused on where AI demand is more broadly flowing, which companies are translating spend into profits, and how to position ahead of the next leadership shift. Viewed through a longer-cycle lens—especially around when monetization becomes meaningful—the expectation that Big Tech will lead as early as the first half of 2026 appears premature relative to other, more attractive areas of the market.

Royston Roche and Damien Robbins, Equity Analysts at Tech Insider Network contributed to this analysis.

Please note: The Tech Insider Network conducts research and draws conclusions for the Fund’s positions. We then share that information with our readers. This is not a guarantee of a stock’s performance. Please consult your personal financial advisor before buying any stock in the companies mentioned in this analysis.

data.title: Underwriting First, Dividends Second: Why RLI's Discipline Finally Earned A Buy Rating
data.description:
RLI Corp. (RLI) is a dividend aristocrat within the insurance sector - one of the more “boring” industries that's not particularly beloved by investors.

Despite the sector’s lack of glamour, there are hidden gems that deliver steady results thanks to disciplined underwriting or strong positions in niche markets. The best‑performing insurance carriers often combine both qualities and return excess capital through dividend increases and share‑repurchase programs.

RLI is a true dividend compounder, having spent nearly five decades rewarding patient and loyal shareholders.

The earnings season has kicked off, and several insurers have already released their FY2025 results, including Travelers (TRV) and Old Republic (ORI). While ORI’s results were not well received by investors, Travelers’ performance exceeded analysts’ expectations.

As for RLI, which recently published its FY2025 results, the outcome was mind-blowing. Or at least, the company has beaten the market's expectations, with a strong fourth-quarter performance.

The last time I covered RLI, my conclusion was the company was richly valued, despite undeniable strengths. As the FY2025 results have been published, it's time to reconsider my opinion, or at least to revisit it by deep-diving into Q4 2025 and FY2025 results.

Underwriting Discipline Adamantly Unchanged

RLI Corp. remains one of the strongest property and casualty (P&C) insurers in the U.S. with a 10-year combined ratio (2025 excluded) of 88.5%.

The insurance carrier's underwriting performance outperformed Chubb's (CB), while I do consider the Evan Greenberg-managed company as the "One". But Chubb is a growing insurer and succeeds in combining premium growth with underwriting discipline.

RLI is another kind of P&C insurer. It's a smaller insurance carrier (ca. $1.5 billion of annual premiums), while Chubb is a behemoth operating in 54 countries and generating almost 50 billion per year ($49.8 billion in 2024, and definitely above 50 billion in 2025).

RLI's underwriting performance is mind-blowing, but it is "easier" (relatively speaking) to report a 80%ish combined ratio when you earn only $1.5+ billion of premiums.

Furthermore, RLI operates in niche markets, such as the surety business, which is highly profitable. In 2025, RLI’s surety segment generated underwriting income of $29.2 million on just $148 million of earned premiums, resulting in a combined ratio of 80.3%.

The property insurance business was even more profitable, with a combined ratio of 57.2% and underwriting gains of $219.1 million. For the non‑insurance fetishists, a company posting a 90% combined ratio is considered a solid insurance carrier. Posting a 40%+ underwriting margin, as RLI’s property segment did, is either nonsense, a miracle, a statistical error, or a combination of all three.

Fortunately, the casualty business - the largest portion of the book - delivers a more standard margin, with a combined ratio of 98.3% in 2025. But keep one thing in mind: the property business generated in 2025 almost half of the earned premiums of the casualty book, yet recorded 13× more underwriting gains. Any CEO, chief actuary, or head of underwriting would kill to have RLI’s property business in their book.

Nonetheless, management remains cautious about the outlook for the property book. As noted during the conference call, premium growth was negative, with an 11% decline, as market conditions for underwriting high‑quality profiles were unfavorable. E&S property premiums decreased by 18%; rates for hurricane coverages were down 15%, and earthquake coverage rates declined 12%. But as noted by Jennifer Klobnak, RLI's Chief Operating Officer:

We have navigated many hard and soft market cycles with discipline and remain focused on securing terms and conditions at an appropriate rate while reducing uncertainty when a loss occurs.

If you need to keep only one sentence from the conference call, it is this one. Only word from this quote: "Discipline".

RLI is an extremely well‑disciplined insurance carrier, focused on underwriting margins rather than premium growth. The company may suffer temporarily from reduced premiums in order to safeguard its margins, but over the long term, RLI will retain high‑quality profiles. The FY2025 underwriting performance was yet another proof of its steady, consistent underwriting discipline. And this underwriting performance will be transformed into dividend increases.

Dividend Gradual Increases With Special Dividend Boosting

RLI is a proud member of the dividend aristocrats, having raised its dividend for 50 straight years. In 2025, the company paid $0.63 per share, a 10% increase from 2024. But RLI also has the habit of boosting its regular dividend by paying a special dividend. In 2025, this special dividend amounted to $2 per share, unchanged from 2024. Since 2010, the company has consistently paid a special dividend, oscillating between $0.50 per share and $7 per share (following the sale of its minority interest in Maui Jim in 2022).

For FY2026, dividend growth will depend heavily on the company’s underwriting performance. However, with a current payout ratio below 20%, shareholders may reasonably expect dividend growth in the range of 5% to 10%, or a quarterly dividend of $0.168 to $0.176 per share. Plus a special dividend ranging from $1 to $2 per share.

Valuation

The last time I covered RLI, the company was valued at 4.3 times the book value. Currently, the insurance carrier is priced 2.8 times the book value and 13 times the earnings.

The company’s valuation looks more appealing, especially in light of the FY2025 results:

  • FY2025 net income of $403.3 million
  • FY2025 combined ratio of 83.6%, representing $264.2 million in underwriting income
  • Book value per share of $19.35, a 33% increase (including dividends) from year‑end 2024

Based on SA’s sector grading, RLI's valuation does not look particularly appealing - or at least it is above the sector median but below its own 5‑year average.

Buy or not Buy? That's the question.

RLI is not cheap; it never will be, except during a market doomsday. But the current market valuation looks more appealing, even though I would prefer acquiring RLI at 1.5 times its book value. That’s certainly a sweet dream. Paying 2.8 times book value is not the bargain of the year. However, if you look only at the P/E multiples, RLI’s valuation appears reasonable - although the company will face potential headwinds in 2026, such as a softer rate environment and elevated inflation. But spoiler alert: all insurers will suffer from those headwinds.

Final Thoughts

RLI delivered steady results in 2025. Although management remains cautious about the 2026 outlook (and so do I, as explained in my 2026 outlook predictions), RLI remains well‑positioned in the P&C sector. Its over‑cycle underwriting margins are high, and underwriting discipline is the company’s DNA.

With a stock price near its 52‑week low, RLI looks appealing. With a 4.6% dividend yield (including the special distribution), almost no debt (OK, only $100 million - almost nothing), and astonishing underwriting resilience, I can’t remain stone‑faced. It’s a timid “BUY,” but it’s still a “BUY.” Yes, I said it (or rather wrote it): it’s a BUY; I’ve upgraded the company’s rating. And the company deserves it. Shareholders won’t triple their capital in one or two years. But if you’re looking for a steady insurer, RLI is your guy (or your gal - no discrimination).

data.title: U.S. Sentiment Slides Again As Consumer Pressures Mount
data.description:
By James Knightley, Chief International Economist, US

Consumer pessimism spreads

The Conference Board measure of January US consumer confidence is surprisingly weak, dropping to 84.5 from an upwardly revised December print of 94.2 and well below the 91.0 consensus prediction. The current conditions index and the expectations component fell by similar amounts with headline sentiment at its weakest since 2014 – so things are apparently worse for households today than they were post the 'Liberation Day' fallout and the pandemic.

Consumer expectations & consumer spending

Yet they keep spending...

It is fair to say that the relationship between sentiment and spending has broken down of late, with sentiment surrounding the economic outlook at levels historically consistent with static spending, whereas real consumer spending expanded at 3.5% growth in 3Q 2025. This divergence likely reflects the narrative that the top 20% of households by income are driving the growth story while the bottom 60% by income are treading water. It is the median household, which is going to be within that bottom 60%, that is reflected in the sentiment survey.

This provides ongoing evidence of the K-shaped story of the US economy. The bottom 60% are worried about their jobs, worried about tariffs squeezing spending power and with Federal Reserve data showing that the bottom 60% of households hold only 15% of total household wealth, they have not benefited much from the surge in property and stock prices. The top 20% of households by income spend more on services, so are not impacted by tariffs to the same degree, have a better sense of job security, and they hold 70% of household wealth. This bifurcation shows little sign of changing in the near term.

Workers suggest more bad news on jobs is coming

One of the key metrics that we look at within this Conference Board consumer confidence report is the jobs plentiful less jobs hard to get measure of how people see the jobs market. Only 23.9% of respondents think "jobs are plentiful" while 20.8% think "jobs are hard to get". The rest are unsure. This gives a net reading of 3.1%, which is the worst outcome (aside from the pandemic) since 2016. The chart suggests that the risk is that unemployment continues ticking higher on the basis that people see and feel changes in the jobs market before they show up in the data.

Unemployment and consumer perceptions of the jobs market

Given this backdrop, it appears to make sense for the Federal Reserve to continue moving monetary policy closer to a neutral setting. They are unlikely to do anything tomorrow, but we continue to look for at least two further rate cuts later in the year.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: Roche: Important De-Risking Of CT-388 In Obesity, Petrelintide Is Next
data.description:
Roche (RHHBY) reported positive phase 2 results of CT-388 in patients with obesity. The results compare well across trials to Eli Lilly’s (LLY) tirzepatide, and while we have to wait for more detailed data presentation at an upcoming medical conference, there should be a sigh of relief for Roche investors as even this topline press release eases tolerability concerns.

As a reminder, there were previously concerns about CT-388's tolerability after high rates of gastrointestinal adverse events in the phase 1 trial, but as I mentioned in my September 2024 article covering the phase 1 data, Roche was pushing the envelope to fully characterize CT-388’s efficacy and safety profile, and the company was planning to address tolerability issues by starting with a low dose followed by measured titration to optimize tolerability.

And while the share price of Roche has advanced 75% since my initiation article in May 2024, I believe these topline results coupled with other shots on goal such as petrelintide, and continued business development efforts put Roche in a good position for significant growth in the obesity and adjacent markets, starting in the late 2020s.

CT-388 looks competitive to tirzepatide on efficacy and tolerability

CT-388 is Roche’s injectable GLP-1/GIP agonist, and it is part of the company’s dual strategy of pursuing injectable and oral treatment options for patients with obesity, type 2 diabetes and related comorbidities.

In my review of the detailed efficacy and safety data of CT-388, I noted the disappointingly high rates of gastrointestinal (‘GI’) adverse events in the phase 1 trial, but that these were driven by design – Roche was pursuing aggressive titration regimens to fully characterize the efficacy, safety, and tolerability profile of both CT-388 and the oral GLP-1 receptor agonist CT-996.

For the phase 2 trial, Roche went with a “start low and go slow” titration regimen with the goal of improving the tolerability of CT-388 compared to what it delivered in the phase 1 trial. And while we need to wait for detailed data presentation at a later date, Roche offered the following comments about safety and tolerability:

  • “The treatment was well-tolerated, with the majority of gastrointestinal-related adverse events being mild-to-moderate, generally consistent with the incretin class of medicines.”
  • “In addition, the treatment discontinuation rate due to adverse events was low (5.9% in CT-388 arms; 1.3% in placebo arm).”

The discontinuation rate looks similar to Eli Lilly’s tirzepatide (Zepbound/Mounjaro) – 4.8-6.7% versus 3.4% in the placebo group in the obesity trial, although the important caveat here is that the comparisons are after 48 weeks of treatment versus 72 weeks for tirzepatide. The table below shows the discontinuation rates of tirzepatide due to adverse events across six SURPASS trials if tirzepatide, and these had follow-up between 40 weeks and 52 weeks, putting them in a comparable range to CT-388.

I still want to see the detailed safety and tolerability data of CT-388 before making more definitive conclusions, but based on the information Roche provided, I believe CT-388’s safety and tolerability will be acceptable as adverse event-based discontinuation rates are well within the ranges of tirzepatide’s rates across several trials.

Moving on to efficacy. With 22.5% weight loss at the highest dose and 18.3% placebo-adjusted based on the treatment estimand, CT-388’s efficacy at week 48 roughly matches the efficacy of tirzepatide’s highest dose of 22% and 18.7% placebo-adjusted at week 72. I drew CT-388's 48-week results on tirzepatide's phase 3 weight loss chart to add perspective.

Importantly, Roche says no weight loss plateau was observed with CT-388 and this leaves room for further improvements in the phase 3 trial.

The caveats to these statements are the requirements for detailed efficacy and safety data and Roche’s phase 3 trial plans. It is possible that the higher doses have unacceptable tolerability and that Roche ends up taking lower and less efficacious doses to phase 3 trials to address the issue.

Overall, the topline results of CT-388 in obese patients look as good as Roche could have expected, and I believe they position the company well for phase 3 trials and CT-388-based combination approaches.

Petrelintide’s phase 2 results represent the next important milestone for Roche’s obesity pipeline, and for partner Zealand Pharma

We should not have to wait much longer for the next important obesity data readout from Roche’s pipeline. The company and partner Zealand Pharma (ZLDPF) will report the phase 2 results of amylin analog petrelintide in obese patients this quarter.

There should be no doubt that this trial will show statistically significant weight loss effects, but the landscape has changed considerably in the last few quarters.

Petrelintide is one of the most advanced amylin analogs, but the bar has moved up from low double-digit weight loss of Novo Nordisk's (NVO) cagrilintide after Eli Lilly delivered impressive weight loss results with eloralintide.

Eloralintide achieved up to 20.2% weight loss in the phase 2 trial, but higher doses were met with worsening GI tolerability. The titrated 3/6/9mg dose is probably the proper benchmark here and it achieved 16.6% weight loss after 48 weeks of treatment.

In the phase 1b trial, petrelintide achieved 8.3-8.6% weight loss after 16 weeks of treatment, and this puts it somewhat below eloralintide's titrated regimen at the same time point.

However, the phase 1b trial of petrelintide enrolled more men versus women (women tend to lose more weight than men) and the average weight at baseline was lower than in the phase 2 trial. The baseline characteristics in the phase 2 trial look more balanced and comparable to eloralintide's, and there is a modest follow-up difference of 42 weeks for petrelintide versus 48 weeks for eloralintide.

This is what I expect to see from petrelintide when Roche and Zealand report phase 2 results in the following weeks:

  • Weight loss of approximately 15-17% at week 42 at higher dose levels.
  • Weight loss of 20% or more is also achievable at higher dose levels, but similar to eloralintide, it may come with worsening tolerability.
  • Safety and tolerability comparable to eloralintide across the dose range.

I expect this dataset will provide sufficient data to fully inform the phase 3 trial of petrelintide monotherapy and that the two companies are well positioned to explore the combination with CT-388. Eli Lilly is doing the same with eloralintide – pursuing phase 3 trials of eloralintide as monotherapy and in combination with tirzepatide and other incretin candidates.

Conclusion

The topline results of CT-388 look as good as Roche could have expected, and I believe they position the company well for phase 3 trials of this candidate as monotherapy and in combination with petrelintide and other potential obesity pipeline candidates.

The focus now turns to amylin analog petrelintide as the phase 2 results are expected this quarter. I expect to see similar efficacy, safety, and tolerability to Eli Lilly’s eloralintide and fast progress towards phase 3 monotherapy trials and combination regimens with CT-388.
